One 2025 paper calculated that, compared with a legislative body that represents all racial groups equally, in the U.S. Senate, white people functionally enjoy 1.14 votes per person while Black people have 0.84 votes, Asian Americans 0.75, and Latinos 0.67.

This form of affirmative action for white people draws little notice but has an important effect. Studies have found that the Senate has a persistent tilt in favor of Republican candidates and conservative-policy outcomes. The tilt may be worse than it appears, given that Republicans have often squandered their structural advantage by nominating extreme candidates who have lost winnable races.

The DSA’s platform sets out an ambition to reduce the Senate to a symbolic institution like the House of Lords, which once exerted counter-majoritarian influence over the United Kingdom but now simply affirms policies enacted by the democratically elected Parliament. That is obviously an ambitious goal with no prospect of fulfillment anytime soon. Even the more modest goal of admitting Washington, D.C., and Puerto Rico as states, which would reduce but not eliminate the Senate’s conservative tilt, is probably not in reach for the next Democratic majority.

But advocating for morally just, utopian policies is the sort of thing that democratic socialists ought to be doing. Republicans want to scare voters into thinking that if Democrats win the midterm elections, the United States will be on the road to a future in which the Senate is no more—or is, at best, an impotent debate club for elderly statesmen. If only!

entries.title: Maine Is a Mess
entries.content:
Kathie Purdy doesn’t mind the phone calls. And there have been a lot of them—producing a sustained buzzing from her iPhone throughout the nearly two weeks since Graham Platner announced that he would drop out of the Maine Senate race. At one point, Purdy’s device grew so hot that she had to take it out of its case to let it breathe. But that was okay, she told me. As chair of the York County Democratic Committee, it was her job to help guide Mainers through this confusing moment.

What Purdy did not appreciate was the insinuation—from other Democrats, from members of the media, from online trolls—that the process to replace Platner was somehow undemocratic. “This is what I’m talking about,” Purdy told me on Sunday, showing me a text from a voter asking whom “leadership had picked” for the nomination. It was early afternoon in the Sanford Performing Arts Center, and we were sitting side by side on the stage, looking out over the democratic process in action. Purdy rolled her eyes, exasperated. “Actually, right now we are conducting the caucus-type meeting in Sanford where the participants vote for a group of delegates,” she replied, using voice-to-text. “That’s the folks who’ll be voting for your Senate candidate.”

Choosing those delegates was not a tidy affair. All morning, the room had been crowded, even though the state party had urged people to please consider voting from home. Mainers who wanted to be delegates and Mainers who came to meet delegate candidates and campaign volunteers proffering lists of allied delegates wove in between the aisles of red-upholstered chairs, shaking hands and arguing and complaining about various parts of the process in which they were taking part. Some struggled with the Wi-Fi. Occasionally, Purdy took the stage to make an announcement: We found a pair of car keys. Please come to the stage if they’re yours!

Looking around, I imagined that, somewhere a few hours north, Susan Collins was rubbing her hands together with delight. Maine Democrats have failed to unseat her for nearly three decades, and there’s plenty of reason to believe that in three months they’ll come up short again. Zoomed out, the situation is, objectively, ridiculous. Maine Democrats nominated Platner despite a bouquet of red flags, including a Nazi tattoo and allegations of volatile behavior from past girlfriends. Now that an accusation of rape has toppled Platner’s candidacy, the Democrats who selected him are desperately trying to pick up the pieces of a race that might be their party’s best shot to take back the Senate.

Yet the predictable conclusion—that the Democrats are once again in disarray—could not be drawn, at least not fully. Because ultimately, after all the hemming and hawing and arguing and complaining, the Democrats of York County sat down and voted. Totals were reached surprisingly quickly. Only a few hours after most attendees had arrived, Purdy was reading out the names of the 84 delegates and 17 alternates who would go to the party’s nominating convention in Bangor.

Plenty of people were frustrated with the results, which, across Maine, were very good for Troy Jackson, a progressive former state legislator. Others had issues with the process itself. But “that’s democracy,” Purdy told me. So far, in Maine, it’s going pretty well.

In the days after Platner announced that he was dropping out of the race, there was much consternation about how the Democrats would replace him. The party wouldn’t have time to conduct another primary. But if state Democratic leaders simply chose a new nominee on their own, voters might be indignant. Much of Platner’s support, after all, had come from people exhausted with the so-called establishment.

The Maine Democrats had settled on a compromise: They would hold a convention on July 25, where 601 delegates would choose Platner’s replacement. Before that, each county would have an election to choose its portion of delegates. People were not exactly pleased about this. Whereas independent Mainers had been able to participate in the June primary, only registered Democrats were allowed to select the replacement nominee. “As an independent you’re saying my vote doesn’t count now,” one commenter on the York County Democratic Committee Facebook page wrote. (Much of the debate has played out on Facebook.) Another self-proclaimed former Democrat weighed in: “If you believe primary voters should choose nominees, not party insiders, consider whether your party registration still reflects your values.”

Meanwhile, the county committee chairs—who, as volunteers, are paid precisely zero dollars for their work—were under a lot of stress. No one from the Maine Democrats returned any of my calls or emails. Purdy was begging people to be kind. “I would really appreciate some respect,” she wrote on Facebook. “Please stop the snark.” Joseph Zamboni, the chair of the Cumberland County Democratic Committee, seemed on the verge of tears when I spoke with him on the phone about where and when the nominating meeting would take place. In one of his many lengthy social-media updates, Zamboni tried to shed some light on the stressful decision-making process. “Nobel Prize–winning scholar Herbert Simon, whose work transformed the fields of economics, psychology, and administration, argued that people rarely make perfectly informed or ‘optimal’ decisions because none of us has unlimited time, information, or mental capacity,” he wrote.

So I did not know what I would find on Sunday. But when I arrived at the auditorium in Sanford at 9 a.m. sharp, I was greeted with a dozen volunteers and a box of donut holes. In the parking lot outside, supporters held a rally for Jackson while, inside, dozens of people were already circulating. “Usually, when we hold a convention, we have to go begging” for delegates, one county committee chair told me, “Right now we’re fighting ’em off.” (In York County, more than 400 people had applied to fill 101 positions.)

For two hours before voting began, people made the case for their candidates. Most were either supporting Jackson, a logger and former state-Senate president, or Nirav Shah, the former leader of the Maine CDC, who has since dropped out of the race. Jackson’s supporters said they liked him because his positions are most closely aligned with those of Platner, and he could, they believe, more easily inherit Platner’s movement. “We’ve lost a lot of momentum, and he has the best chance of keeping them engaged,” Reed Gordon, a 29-year-old delegate candidate, told me. The case for Shah was that he is slightly more moderate and might, therefore, be an easier sell for independent and moderate Mainers. Platner was too aggressive, Paula Busby, a 62-year-old from the town of Lebanon, told me. “We need to stand up against” President Trump, she said, “but we have to be measured.”

Because the Democrats at these county meetings were choosing delegates rather than voting directly for candidates themselves, campaigns had made lists of allied delegates ahead of time and passed them out. Delegate selection was, basically, a proxy election: If you wanted Jackson to win, you’d vote for Jackson’s slate. This was upsetting to the Mainers who’d hoped that the people sent to the convention would be impartial. “This isn’t the way I’d have done it,” Julian Rogers, the former vice chair of the Maine Democrats and a delegate candidate himself, told me. “Rather than sort of coronating one person, I think we need to hear from these other people and have a little more balance.”

Leah Macomber, a game developer from Saco, was also on the ballot to be a delegate, but she didn’t know anything about the slates until Sunday morning. “I was so pissed off,” she told me. “I’m a Mainer, and I don’t like being told what to do, or especially who to vote for.” So she made a T-shirt reading Vote Independent Thinker and wore it to the event. She also handed out lollipops labeled Susan sucks to try to stand out to voters. Neither she nor Rogers were selected as delegates.

Still, even they were impressed at how quickly the process had been put into place, and how clear the communication had been from the state and county parties. It was preferable, some Mainers told me, to the 2024 process to replace Joe Biden, which was ultimately an anointing of Kamala Harris. And it was obviously better than powering through with a toxic nominee.

The Democrats got a redo, a freebie, to try again and get someone better. “I was so disappointed when Platner dropped out,” Diana Garry, a retired nurse from Shapleigh, told me. “I was so discouraged. But now I’m thinking it’s a blessing.”

By 11:15 a.m., people were in their seats, with their phones and iPads open, ready to vote. “It’ll all be electronic, so it’ll be fast,” Purdy said from the stage. I wondered whether she was tempting fate. The Democrats, after all, do not have the best track record when it comes to using technology to facilitate important elections. And when Purdy began reading out instructions for voting through the link sent via email—“To edit your submission, hit ‘Continue,’ then ‘Edit,’ then ‘Continue’ again”—a feeling of dread came over me. It was not assuaged by the line for technical support that quickly formed down the center aisle of the auditorium.

But my concerns proved unfounded. The vote was going smoothly. An hour in, all of the people in line had been helped. Some Mainers had left; they’d see the results posted online. Soon, Purdy was onstage giving a 10-minute warning. “Can I get a ‘yay’?” she asked the now-dwindling crowd. “Yay!” people replied, more enthusiastically than I’d expected.

She sat back down next to me. “I’m proud of what we’ve done,” she said, looking relieved. “We had a very short time to do something herculean.” Thanks to Sunday’s elections, next weekend’s convention will be pretty straightforward. The question now is whether Purdy and all the rest of Maine’s Democrats can pull off something similarly herculean in November.

entries.title: War by Any Other Name
entries.summary: No classified briefings, no press conferences, no acknowledgment that the conflict with Iran is back on
entries.content:
No classified briefings, no press conferences, no acknowledgment that the conflict with Iran is back on

The Trump administration is back at war. It just doesn’t want to admit it.

In the 11 days since the United States resumed its air strikes against Iran, neither President Trump nor any other senior administration official has publicly addressed the campaign. U.S. forces began by striking Iranian military facilities but later expanded to attacking civilian infrastructure that the administration says is being used for military purposes. As of today, congressional officials had yet to receive classified briefings on the latest phase of the war, aides told us.

The Pentagon, whose leadership stepped in front of the cameras in the weeks following the start of the war, in late February, hasn’t held a press briefing since May to answer even basic questions: Why is a resumption of the war necessary? What are the strategic objectives, and how have they changed? How many casualties have American forces suffered? What would define success?

The president, who has repeatedly claimed the war was already won, scheduled a televised address to the nation last week. Instead of talking about the war, though, he focused on election security and mentioned Iran only in passing, telling the nation that “we are likewise winning big in Iran, and you will see the fruits of that labor very, very shortly.” (This is hard to square with the record of the past several months.)

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, were on Capitol Hill this afternoon to plead for $67.1 billion for emergency military funding to replenish munitions and meet other needs, on top of the Pentagon’s $1.5 trillion regular budget request. Neither uttered the word Iran in their opening statement. Hegseth, in his testimony, referred to the war as “an operation.”

Even the deaths of American troops have been treated as though they belong to another conflict. After two U.S. service members were killed in Jordan by an Iranian missile on Friday, the Pentagon initially said that they died supporting Operation Inherent Resolve, the decade-plus campaign against the Islamic State. It then issued another statement saying that the two troops, along with a third who was killed in Iraq when an attack drone detonated, died while “supporting overseas operations”—but still did not explicitly mention Operation Epic Fury, the Pentagon’s name for the conflict with Iran. (Another service member, who had been reported missing in Jordan, is believed dead, the Pentagon said today.)

With those deaths included, at least 18 U.S. service members have been killed in combat since the war started—more than were killed in combat in Afghanistan in 2016, 2017, and 2018 each, and only slightly fewer than the total recorded during the 12 years of Operation Inherent Resolve against ISIS. At least 1,700 Iranians have been killed, according to Iranian officials, including dozens over the past 10 days.

Trump is scheduled to attend the dignified-transfer service for the slain soldiers at Dover Air Force Base tomorrow. But his brief remarks saluting the fallen soldiers were quickly followed by his usual blustery insistence that the war was going well. Trump has lashed out on social media against Iran, vowing to punish its regime for killing Americans. But a senior official acknowledged to us that initial intelligence assessments had revealed that the new wave of U.S. strikes has likely done little to change Iran’s behavior.

Trump expected a quick victory, and so the military didn’t prepare for a prolonged conflict. There was no monthslong buildup of forces and no large-scale deployment of troops. Although the military surged additional forces into the region, many assets—particularly Navy ships—were diverted from other war theaters rather than trained for this conflict. The Pentagon has since extended the deployments of nearly half of those ships, further taxing an already-strapped force.

The U.S. teamed up with Israel in launching the war but didn’t ask for support beforehand from other allies. No country has offered to step in for U.S. forces or share the burden of Trump’s blockade of the Strait of Hormuz and the protection of U.S. troops in the region.

On Capitol Hill, Hegseth and Caine didn’t get a warm reception for their request that taxpayers cover the cost of “certain aspects” of the war, which they put at $37.5 billion.

The administration “chose to break every rule and said that everyone before them was stupid for using a playbook of talking to the nation, talking to allies, and setting goals,” one former senior military officer, who requested anonymity out of fear of retaliation, told us. “And now we are in a war with no good options and no U.S. and international support. It’s a bit of a trap of our own making.” Given that there appears to be no escape from that trap other than acknowledging defeat, the administration is expected to keep fighting, hoping that doing so leads to a better outcome.

Even some of Trump’s closest allies privately acknowledged that he is in a bind. His choice: Escalate the conflict and risk losing more American soldiers and depleting stockpiles of munitions while hurting Republicans’ election chances this fall, or walk away now to cut his political losses while leaving Iran in a stronger strategic position than it has held in decades. That would be the sort of humiliation that Trump loathes. But one Trump ally pointed us to a shift in rhetoric on Fox News; in recent days, a number of influential hosts, including Laura Ingraham, have floated the possibility of abandoning the war in an eleventh-hour effort to help the GOP at the ballot box. Trump, the ally noted, often uses his favorite cable channel as a gauge of public opinion.

Two people close to the president told us that Trump may at some point consider that sort of exit. But for now, despite the growing costs, he is continuing the fight. While sitting next to the leader of Lebanon in the Oval Office today, the president seemed to commit himself to the type of forever war he once derided: “We’re not finished at all,” Trump told reporters. “We’re not leaving right now.” He also said the United States is planning to bomb Pickaxe Mountain on the suspicion that it houses part of Iran’s nuclear program.

Military planners often say that hope is not a strategy—and neither is fighting a war while treating it as something less than that. One likely result is a protracted back-and-forth from which the U.S. ultimately retreats, hundreds of billions of dollars later. “This pickup game has turned into a marathon that we never mobilized for in the first place,” Bryan Clark, a senior fellow at Hudson Institute and an expert on naval operations, told us. “Now we’re having to mobilize for it on the fly.”

The administration’s apparent objective is to raise Iran’s cost of disrupting traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, which was an open waterway until Tehran closed it in the early days of the war. Iran, meanwhile, is trying to raise the cost of the U.S. military presence in the region while using force to block ships from transiting the strait without its permission. Earlier today, a U.K. maritime agency said that a tanker operating in the strait was struck by an unknown projectile, leading its crew to abandon ship. Iran has also increased its targeting of U.S. naval vessels, American officials told us; both countries continue climbing the ladder of escalation.

As the war has dragged on, Tehran appears to have adapted. In the early days, Iran was able to overwhelm U.S. air defenses with relatively cheap drones. But more and more, the country is turning to cheap, quickly produced ballistic missiles. These missiles travel faster than drones and can do far more damage. In the span of 24 hours last week, Iran launched at least three ballistic missiles toward Jordan. One video from the attacks shows a missile striking a Jordanian base that the U.S. operates in, and no apparent attempt at interception, fueling concerns inside the Pentagon that commanders are conserving air-defense munitions at the expense of troop protection. Hegseth told the Hill on Tuesday that “everything our troops need on the battlefield, they’re getting in spades and beyond.” But the military also urgently needs more funds to replenish its stockpiles.

Over the course of the conflict, Trump has let deadline after deadline slide, while Iran has learned not to fear his bellicose threats. The president, his advisers have told us, understands that the war is hurting him politically but rages against those who say that his proposed deal on Iran’s nuclear program is weaker than the one President Obama brokered a decade before.

Houthi rebels in Yemen, who are backed by Iran, announced yesterday their intention to get off the war’s sidelines and close portions of the Red Sea to shipping, which could further inflame the global energy crisis. If anything, the war shows signs of broadening, not ending, whatever the White House says—or doesn’t say.

entries.title: J. D. Vance Isn’t a Fool. Why Does He Act Like One?
entries.summary: Every politician does some pandering, but few do it as clumsily, or with such evident disdain for their own supporters, as the vice president.
entries.content:
Every politician does some pandering, but few do it as clumsily, or with such evident disdain for their own supporters, as the vice president.

This is an edition of The Atlantic Daily, a newsletter that guides you through the biggest stories of the day, helps you discover new ideas, and recommends the best in culture. Sign up for it here.

Updated 10:22 a.m. ET on July 22, 2026

When J. D. Vance announced the title of his new book, some bookworms noticed a curious convergence. The vice president’s first book is Hillbilly Elegy, a title that closely parallels Appalachian Elegy, a poetry collection by the radical Black feminist author bell hooks. Communion, his second volume, shares its name, though not its subject matter, with another hooks work (hers is on “the female search for love,” and his discusses his conversion to Catholicism).

Many people seemed incredulous at the idea that Vance would be familiar with hooks, but I’m not so sure. Even if Vance has never read hooks, he could very easily be aware of her. (He has previously declined to comment on the titles’ similarities.) Vance climbed from an impoverished, troubled childhood to graduate with honors from the Ohio State University and then attend Yale Law School. People who manage such rises are typically intellectual sponges, hoovering up knowledge to make up ground on more privileged classmates. Accounts of Vance’s time in law school, including stories of his close friendship with a transgender classmate, all point to a young man with an open mind, broad interests, and knowledge.



This makes it peculiar to watch Vance work so hard to appear dumb these days. Liberal pundits used to describe former House Speaker Newt Gingrich as “a dumb person’s idea of a smart person.” Vance appears to be performing a smart person’s idea of a dumb person. During an interview with Joe Rogan last week, as my colleague Helen Lewis reported, Vance acted perplexed that people had been so offended when a UFC fighter, competing in a fight at the White House, called former First Lady Michelle Obama a man. Rogan was left to provide some explanation.

In June, Vance feigned ignorance about the fact that Democrats are more likely than Republicans to vote by mail, a tendency that produces the familiar pattern known as the “blue shift” in election tallies. In May, during a White House briefing, he argued that congressional Democrats’ applause for King Charles III during a joint meeting of Congress proved that “No Kings” rallies critical of Trump were hypocritical. “Maybe they don’t care so much about kings as they pretend that they do,” he said. (Yes, he said this with a very straight face. See for yourself.) “Maybe they just don’t like the agenda that we’re implementing that’s actually making American workers and American families safe and prosperous again.”

He’s been at this for some time. In 2025, on the comedian Theo Von’s podcast, Vance said, “I feel like something happened, like, 10 years ago, where every—it’s like you have to think that every single person who fought for the Confederate side was an evil person. I just think that’s so stupid.” That would be stupid, if anyone were arguing it. In reality, the big debate has been about questions such as whether military bases should be named for traitors and whether governments should host monuments to a rebellion to defend slavery. In 2024, Vance told what he quickly acknowledged were falsehoods about Haitian immigrants in Springfield, Ohio, to bring attention to administration talking points about immigration.

Separately, Vance has developed a conspiratorial mode of thinking—a plague on many otherwise smart people. Despite having access to some of the best information in the world, he reportedly became convinced that a broad plot was behind Charlie Kirk’s assassination, alarming his wife; Vance suggested on Rogan’s show that Jeffrey Epstein had shadowy government connections in Israel or the United States. (Perhaps Vance was thinking of Epstein’s ties to Donald Trump or Howard Lutnick?)

Why is a smart man spending so much time saying stupid things? The simplest explanation is that Vance believes his voters are stupid. Or as Vance himself told Rogan last week: “There’s a real populism that I’m very much a fan of because I think you should be responsive to people, but there’s, like, a faux populism of: The way that I’m going to appeal to people is by assuming that they’re idiots and acting like they’re idiots.”

This is admission by analysis. All politicians pander, but the best of them do it with some finesse. Vance, who struggles through the most basic photo ops, is not one of the best; his two successful elections are both testaments to Trump’s political muscle. In 2022, when he was a U.S. Senate candidate, I included Vance in a round-up of very intelligent Republicans who were clumsily pretending to be fools for electoral purposes. Vance seems to have doubled down on the strategy since then, but each new incident just offers more evidence of his dripping disdain for voters’ intelligence.

This may not be the wisest political strategy, H. L. Mencken notwithstanding. Vance’s approval ratings have been underwater for more than a year, and the Never Trump pollster Sarah Longwell recently conducted a focus group with Trump voters and found that Vance has left them cold. Either way, Vance’s performance plays as tragicomedy: comic because he’s so bad at it, but tragic because of who Vance was not that long ago. Much has been made of his critique of Trump in his 2016 Atlantic essay, and although his flip-flop on the president is astonishing, he has also reversed himself on a more fundamental view of politics.

In that essay, he criticized the demagogic tendency to condescend to people in so-called flyover country, where he grew up—to view them as yokels who could be easily manipulated with simplistic talking points and grievance plays. The harms that Trump identified, Vance wrote, “demand serious thought and measured action—from governments, yes, but also from community leaders and individuals.” Trump, he argued, was cynically co-opting those harms for his own gain and doing Americans a disservice. That Vance was right; this Vance doesn’t seem to care.

Related:

Here are four new stories from The Atlantic:

Today’s News

  • The United States and Iran continued exchanging strikes for the tenth night in a rowas fears grew that the conflict could further disrupt global oil supplies. Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthis threatened shipping in the Red Sea yesterday, sending oil prices above $90 a barrel, the highest price in more than a month.
  • New Jersey Governor Mikie Sherrill said that from June 2023 to June 2024, a software error at the state’s motor-vehicle agency had improperly registered about 6,600 noncitizens to vote, and roughly 400 later cast ballots in unspecified elections. Sherill said that these voters had correctly identified themselves as a noncitizen when they registered at the agency, and that they will be removed from the voter rolls.
  • President Trump imposed 50 percent tariffs on most Canadian goodsyesterday, including products protected under the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement, arguing that Canada unfairly discriminates against American autos, alcohol, and dairy products.

Evening Read

Does Your Credit Card Even Plunk?

By Alexandra Tremayne-Pengelly

Physical credit cards should be a thing of the past. Over the past few years, touchless services such as Apple Pay have driven a boom in the digital-payment industry: Digital wallets now account for about half of global online purchases and one-third of point-of-sale payments. You would think that credit-card companies might abandon investing in cards that weigh down your wallet—but you’d be wrong.

More From The Atlantic

Culture Break

Read. Amitav Ghosh believes that climate change requires rethinking the novel. His new work of fiction, Ghost-Eye, fails that standard, Robert Rubsam argues.

Explore. Dua Lipa’s latest literary cause is good PR—and a complicated topic, Sophia Nguyen writes.

This article previously misstated that the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement is expired; in fact, it remains in effect but was not renewed.

Rafaela Jinich contributed to this newsletter.

entries.title: Iraq’s Man in the Middle
entries.summary: The country’s new prime minister is trying to navigate between Washington and Tehran.
entries.content:
The country’s new prime minister is trying to navigate between Washington and Tehran.

Every Iraqi prime minister since the fall of Saddam Hussein has inherited the same impossible job: Convince Washington that Iraq is not slipping entirely into Iran’s orbit while convincing Tehran that it is not becoming an American outpost.

Iraq’s new prime minister now faces that challenge under the most trying of circumstances. The United States and Iran are at war—and Iraq sits between them. When I met with Ali al-Zaidi during his visit to Washington, D.C., last week, which included an Oval Office audience with President Trump, I was looking for signs that Iraq’s strategy had changed. Instead, I heard a familiar refrain: fewer American troops; more American business.

“Military presence does not create ties between societies,” he told me in a rare interview, conducted under heavy security in downtown Washington. “Economic engagement does.”

It’s an aspiration that’s running up against a challenging reality. Roughly 2,000 American troops remain in his country, advising Iraqi security forces in their fight against the Islamic State. Meanwhile, dozens of Iran-backed militias operate across Iraq, despite repeated government promises to bring them under state control. As of now, the U.S. troops are set to leave by the end of September, bringing a symbolic end to America’s war in Iraq after 23 years. Many experts I spoke with are skeptical, however, noting that if fighting continues to escalate between Washington and Tehran after the collapse of the recent cease-fire, those troops are strategically positioned to bolster America’s Middle Eastern presence—even if they are also among the most exposed to attack.

Around the time al-Zaidi and I spoke in Washington, three U.S. service members were reported dead in incidents in Jordan and Iraq, demonstrating how quickly Iraq can once again become a focal point of conflict. Trump “had the inclination to deliver a severe blow” to Iran after days of tit-for-tat strikes, al-Zaidi told me, though he declined to elaborate on what that might entail, or whether it involved ground troops. He would say only that Trump had asked for his opinion, and he advised to delay retaliation.

“Trump expressed his frustration, saying that despite the agreement reached with the Iranians, they failed to honor it—a sentiment echoed by the Iranians regarding the U.S.,” he said. “You can always resort to force later, but negotiation is what matters.” (A U.S. official didn’t address the exchange between Trump and al-Zaidi, but said in a statement that “these devastating blows will continue until the President deems otherwise.” The official added that talks between the U.S. and Iran continue.)

Over the past two decades, Iran has ratcheted up its influence in Iraq through political parties, economic ties, and armed groups affiliated with its Popular Mobilization Forces. The U.S., meanwhile, provides military training and intelligence support against the remnants of ISIS, and plays a key role in Iraq’s access to the global financial system through the dollar-based banking network. U.S. officials told me Iraqi intelligence played a key role in the U.S. operation that killed Iranian General Qassem Soleimani outside the airport in Baghdad in January 2020, a military achievement from Trump’s first term that he still celebrates. (Iraq has denied any involvement.) Yet that strike triggered a crisis in U.S.-Iraq relations, fueling demands from powerful Iraqi factions to expel American forces altogether.

Iraq matters to Washington for reasons that extend far beyond Baghdad. American troops stationed there provide logistical and intelligence support to Iraqi security forces, making their bases frequent targets whenever tensions escalate with Iran. Every escalation raises the same question: Can Baghdad disarm the militias, most of which take their orders from Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps? Al-Zaidi repeatedly insisted, his voice rising with anger, that “there is no option except surrendering weapons.” He is not prepared to accept a failed state.

On almost every issue with Iraq that has frustrated Washington for two decades—corruption, militias, sovereignty, and Iranian influence—al-Zaidi claimed the situation was changing. Yet each pledge echoed promises made by nearly every Iraqi prime minister before him. He acknowledged as much, but insisted that some of his early crackdowns on corrupt public servants have succeeded, indicating that this time is different.

At 41 years old, al-Zaidi is the youngest Iraqi prime minister. A political outsider and multimillionaire businessman, he belongs to a generation shaped by the wars that remade modern Iraq. He was born during the Iran-Iraq War of the 1980s, when Saddam sought to take advantage of the political chaos in Iran following its 1979 Islamic Revolution. Saddam ordered an invasion of Iran the following year on the assumption that Tehran’s military had been depleted by the domestic upheaval. But Iraqi forces faced stiff Iranian resistance, and the war dragged on for about eight years, killing hundreds of thousands of soldiers on both sides and devastating both economies.

Today’s confrontation, between the United States and Israel on one side and Iran on the other, has made commercial shipping and global energy supplies central targets, with oil tankers again caught in the crossfire, as they were 40 years ago. When I asked al-Zaidi what Iraq had learned from its own war with Iran, his answer was striking: “Even the winner loses,” he said, while taking a drag from his vape.

The answer reflected Iraq’s experience. The Iran-Iraq War ended without a decisive victor but left Iraq heavily indebted, politically weakened, and socially scarred. Those burdens helped set the stage for Saddam’s invasion of Kuwait in August 1990, which triggered the Gulf War and, ultimately, the chain of events that culminated in the 2003 U.S. invasion of Iraq, when al-Zaidi was 18 years old. The removal of Saddam by the U.S. was followed by the rise of militias with deep ideological, security, and economic dependence on Tehran.

Iraq, al-Zaidi told me, should be “a meeting point, not a place for hostility” between the U.S. and Iran. His country can bridge the divide by getting U.S. forces to leave and then by ensuring that any rogue militia groups disarm. He insisted that economic opportunity is the answer to Iraq’s problems: If people feel they have a future, they won’t turn to warfare or sectarianism. He acknowledged he will likely face resistance and said he could die trying to make it happen. “We will accept nothing less,” he told me.

Throughout our conversation, which was in Arabic, al-Zaidi sounded less like a career politician than a chief executive pitching a turnaround strategy. Iraq has resources. America has capital and technology. Replace soldiers with investors, and the relationship becomes sustainable.

But signs of recovery last year have been snuffed out by the latest conflict, which has disrupted trade routes, strained public finances, and prompted the International Monetary Fund to forecast that Iraq’s economy will shrink by 6.8 percent this year. Al-Zaidi pointed to new energy agreements signed in Washington as evidence that investors should look beyond the war. But he also acknowledged that his country’s economy is suffering greatly from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, losing about $50 billion in revenue because of halted oil exports, he said.

Al-Zaidi emerged as a compromise candidate for prime minister in late April after months of political deadlock. He has vowed to forgo a government salary, avoid forming a political party, and serve only one four-year term. Since taking office, he has paired those promises with high-profile anti-corruption raids, including the June arrest of dozens of officials, among them the deputy oil minister, inside Baghdad’s Green Zone, the heavily fortified district in central Baghdad now controlled by the Iraqi government.

Before politics, he built a business empire spanning agriculture, logistics, and energy. A bank where he served as chairman later lost access to U.S.-dollar transactions after American authorities raised concerns over alleged Iran-linked money laundering. Trump, who described al-Zaidi as “handsome” when they met last week, appears receptive to a leader who speaks in the language of investment rather than ideology. Al-Zaidi pitches Iraq, first and foremost, as a business opportunity, though he avoids the term deal, saying investment will naturally follow because the two countries want to work together.

“We are discussing an investment partnership between Iraq and the U.S. that would span 30 years,” he added. “The fighting could cease in months, days, or even hours—that rests with the politicians.”

Al-Zaidi’s own rise to power reflects Washington’s enduring influence. After months of political paralysis, former Prime Minister Nouri al-Maliki, an influential hard-liner with close ties to Iran, was pushed aside earlier this year after Trump publicly threatened to cut U.S. assistance if al-Maliki returned to office. Al-Zaidi emerged as the alternative because he was acceptable to Iraq’s competing political factions and, just as important, was unlikely to face resistance from Washington. His challenge now is to prove that political compromise can translate into actual authority over a state where many of the most heavily armed actors still operate outside government control.

Al-Zaidi has pledged that by September 30, independent armed factions will either surrender their weapons or be absorbed into Iraq’s official security forces. The coming months will determine whether al-Zaidi’s vision survives contact with the region’s oldest reality: Iraq rarely gets to choose the wars that shape its future.

entries.title: Where Did All the Computer-Science Professors Go?
entries.summary: AI companies are stripping universities of their best researchers.
entries.content:
AI companies are stripping universities of their best researchers.

Anthropic has poached such an array of high-profile professors that it has become a punch line in academia. “‘I’m joining Anthropic’ is the new meme right now,” Subbarao Kambhampati, a computer-science professor at Arizona State University (who has not joined Anthropic), told us. This month, the AI company hired the chair of UC Berkeley’s department of electrical engineering and computer science, presumably to help build more capable bots. Perhaps more surprisingly, Anthropic has in recent weeks also picked up a Stanford economist, a theoretical physicist from the University of Maryland, and an analytic philosopher from UT Austin.

AI companies are turning into something like mini-universities in their own right. OpenAI employs top mathematicians and physicists—including one who studies black holes, and another who specializes in string theory. At least three computer-science professors joined Meta’s AI lab in late June. DeepMind, like Anthropic, is home to a crew of philosophers. And Anthropic’s recent job postings indicate an interest in hiring legal scholars and political scientists. It’s unclear exactly how many current and former professors are working at AI companies. Across these four firms, we found more than 80—the majority of whom are computer scientists. Some have left academia entirely; others are still working part-time at a university. That number is likely a significant undercount, because we don’t have access to internal data; it also doesn’t include the many professors who have started their own companies, those at other AI start-ups, and those working with the industry in a less formal capacity.

Particularly for AI researchers, these companies have a strong gravitational pull. “Much of the important research is being done in industry now,” Humphrey Shi, a computer-science professor at Georgia Tech who joined Nvidia as a vice president last fall, told us. As he sees it, “If you want to do something that really, truly matters, you probably want to join one of those entities.” Tech firms are making offers—including very enticing salaries—that are hard for academics to refuse. In the process, research that previously would have happened in the open is getting locked up behind closed doors.

For decades, universities were the center of AI research. The field itself officially began at a gathering of researchers at Dartmouth in 1956, and federal funding provided much of the field’s early support. In the early 2010s, Silicon Valley executives began to take AI’s commercial potential more seriously, and set out to hire the best researchers. In 2013, Google paid $44 million to acquire a start-up run by a trio of AI researchers from the University of Toronto. Facebook then hired Yann LeCun, an NYU professor, to establish the company’s AI-research lab; Uber poached some 40 Carnegie Mellon researchers to work on driverless cars. But for the most part, even as more work was being done inside of private companies, many of the newly hired academics at tech companies retained their professorships and established a culture of open research. “Researchers will be strongly encouraged to publish their work,” OpenAI wrote in its founding announcement. (Note the organization’s name.) This norm helped lead to the current AI boom: In 2017, scientists at Google published a research paper that was immediately of interest to OpenAI. Google’s innovation, called the “transformer,” is what the T in ChatGPT stands for.

As AI has taken center stage, Silicon Valley has intensified its efforts to recruit star researchers—and looked beyond computer-science departments. Philosophers help train tech companies’ bots to better interact with humans, and economists study the labor-market implications of AI. Compensation is only part of the draw. The current era of AI research requires massive amounts of computing power. Universities have only a fraction of the resources that Silicon Valley can provide, and the chasm has widened as the Trump administration has cut back on scientific funding. Anca Dragan, a UC Berkeley computer scientist who heads DeepMind’s AI-safety-and-alignment department, wrote to us that she was partly motivated to take the job to acquire “the data, compute, and budget access to make progress on safety at the frontier.” Some professors who remain in academia are also forming partnerships with frontier labs or starting their own AI companies, in part so that they can pursue their research without resource constraints. And in some cases, would-be star Ph.D. students are dropping out of their graduate programs—or skipping them altogether—to pursue careers in AI instead.

Although plenty of research is still happening within universities, many professors told us, the result is a flywheel: As more academics get sucked up by industry, the center of AI research moves further from academia, thus increasing the incentive for remaining researchers to leave. The exodus could have some advantages. Many of the professors currently at the labs are on temporary leave; some will likely return to academia full-time. When they do so, they’ll bring new knowledge about frontier research to their institutions. (Dragan is currently preparing to teach a small Ph.D. seminar on AI safety.) Scientists at AI labs might also innovate at a faster pace. After all, they don’t have to worry about applying for grants or waiting years for an article to go through peer review. “If some of the best research is being done in these companies, then I kind of want colleagues and faculty members to be there,” Chris Gregg, a Stanford computer scientist, told us. “You go where the best research is being done, and if that happens to be at a company, so be it.”

But when academics decamp to AI labs or spend more of their time working on their own companies, universities are left with fewer professors to teach the next generation of researchers. (Gregg said that students occasionally ask why courses are no longer offered. The answer is sometimes that the professor is on leave at an AI company.) When star professors leave universities, it becomes harder for students to do work that helps them stand out to prospective employers, Shi said. To differentiate themselves from other applicants, it helps to have worked with mentors who are doing consequential or cutting-edge research. Jennifer Chayes, the dean of the College of Computing, Data Science, and Society at UC Berkeley, told us she fears that as research concentrates in labs with proprietary models, it will be harder for people outside the labs to use AI models to advance science. “Computer-science departments at universities will survive this,” she said. “I don’t know if our innovation economy will.”

As the AI race intensifies, companies now decline to release much of their research for fear of giving away their competitive advantage. Both Gregg and Chayes said they hear from researchers who have gone to AI labs that they can’t publish the work they want to. “The top few AI companies share some things that are at the cutting edge, but it’s a very narrow slice of the potential AI literature that could exist,” Nathan Lambert, an independent AI researcher, told us. According to one study, when AI experts permanently transition from universities to companies, they publish roughly 65 percent fewer papers a year. Papers that do get published tend to emphasize progress on safety efforts and not frontier AI capabilities; corporate research can help fuel marketing hype. And with competition among AI companies escalating, it’s not only the research that is getting locked down, but AI models too. Last month, when Anthropic released a new powerful model called Fable, the company announced that it would invisibly degrade the model’s ability to do certain AI research. The move was positioned as a safety decision, but the resulting outcry from academia was strong. (Anthropic apologized and changed the policy.)

Academics inside of the tech companies are more sanguine. Shi, the Georgia Tech professor who works at Nvidia, said the company is committed to open research, which is partly why he took the job. Meanwhile, Dragan, the Berkeley computer scientist at DeepMind, explained that publishing isn’t everything. “I find a much bigger priority than publishing is engaging in policy and regulation,” she wrote to us. We also reached out to spokespeople at OpenAI, Meta, and Anthropic for comment. Anthropic declined to comment; OpenAI and Meta did not respond.

The implications are potentially profound, not just for AI research, but for science more generally. In Silicon Valley, there’s a popular view that leading AI labs will end up as the primary drivers of science. “AGI has the potential to be the ultimate tool for advancing science and medicine,” DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis wrote last week. Hassabis has previously said that an inspiration for his company is Bell Labs, the R&D arm of AT&T, which employed some 1,200 Ph.D.s at its height in the 1960s. Scientists at the lab earned at least 10 Nobel Prizes, and invented the transistor and the modern solar cell. Already, DeepMind has done Nobel Prize–winning research on protein-structure prediction, and the top models have already demonstrated impressive capabilities in mathematics research. On Sunday, a mathematician who works at Anthropic posted that he had used the company’s Fable model during the World Cup final to resolve a nearly 90-year-old mathematical problem.

But if scientific talent and computing power becomes concentrated inside private firms, Silicon Valley could also end up as a gatekeeper of science. Some are raising concerns: A group of academics recently published a declaration warning about “the increasing involvement of technology companies in mathematical research.” If left unchecked, they argued, the incursion of tech companies into research could affect “the scope and depth of mathematical research itself.” Instead of accelerating science, as the leaders of AI companies claim they will, they might end up suffocating it.
